nativeRange.habitat: Rapids, riffles, and fast-moving shallow streams with large smooth boulders and cobbles; also documented in deeper stream pools. Substrate primarily rock and gravel with little sediment. Water is cool, highly oxygenated, and clear.

decorPreferences: smooth river rocks and rounded cobbles
flat slate or flagstone pieces for resting
powerhead or wavemaker for strong directional flow
minimal fine substrate (sand or fine gravel acceptable)

diet.notes: Primarily grazes biofilm, aufwuchs (periphyton), soft green algae, and diatoms from rock surfaces. Cannot subsist on algae wafers alone — mature biofilm from seasoned rockwork is essential. Will accept sinking algae wafers, Repashy gel foods (Soilent Green or similar), blanched zucchini or cucumber, and frozen spirulina brine shrimp. Has also been documented eating small benthic invertebrates; some sources classify it as omnivorous, but plant material and biofilm dominate its natural diet. May decline and starve in a new tank lacking established biofilm.

goodWith: small peaceful cyprinids (danios, rasboras, white cloud mountain minnows)
other peaceful community fish tolerant of cool, high-flow conditions
avoidWith: other hillstream loach species of similar body shape (territorial competition)
slow-water species unable to cope with strong flow

summary: The Reticulated Hillstream Loach (Sewellia lineolata) is a specialized stream fish from Vietnam and neighboring countries, adapted to fast-moving mountain streams. It requires cool water, powerful flow, and mature biofilm on rocks to thrive, and is best kept in a dedicated hillstream setup.
careNotes:
The Reticulated Hillstream Loach is a highly specialized hillstream fish that demands dedicated setup conditions — it generally does not thrive in a standard community tank. Three requirements stand out as essential. First, water temperature must be kept consistently between 20–24°C; temperatures above 24°C have been reported to cause rapid decline, and a chiller or cool room may be necessary in warm climates. Second, strong, high-volume water flow is needed to replicate the rapids these fish come from — a turnover of 15–20× per hour is commonly cited, achieved with powerheads or wavemakers directed across smooth rock surfaces. Third, mature biofilm must be established on rocks before the fish are introduced; biofilm is their primary food source and takes weeks to develop in a new tank.

The species is generally peaceful toward dissimilar tankmates, but males are somewhat territorial with each other. Keeping a single individual or a group of three or more is generally advised over a pair, as one fish may bully the other in a two-specimen setup. Other hillstream loach genera with similar body shapes, such as Gastromyzon and Hypergastromyzon, may compete for the same rock-surface territories.

The Reticulated Hillstream Loach has a flattened, ray-like body with enlarged pectoral and pelvic fins that allow it to grip smooth rock surfaces in strong current. These fish are accomplished climbers and have been known to exit tanks through small gaps — a tight-fitting lid is strongly advised.

Running the tank for 6–8 weeks before adding fish, with rock surfaces exposed to light, is generally advised to grow sufficient periphyton (the biofilm layer of algae and microorganisms that forms on submerged rocks). Once established, supplement the natural biofilm with algae wafers, gel foods, and blanched vegetables. The species is listed as IUCN Vulnerable (VU) as of 2010; most trade specimens are wild-collected, so sourcing captive-bred stock where available is encouraged.

Copper sensitivity: as with many members of the family Gastromyzontidae, copper-based medications should be avoided; use copper-free treatments instead.
breedingNotes: Breeding in home aquaria has been documented and is considered achievable with patience. Eggs are deposited in gravel substrate interstices and receive no parental care. Fry consume infusoria initially, then accept Artemia nauplii and microworms as they grow. A mature tank with significant mulm and biofilm in the substrate supports fry survival.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Primary cross-check source. Confirms temperature 20–24°C, pH 6.0–7.5, adult size 55–65 mm, family Gastromyzontidae, order Cypriniformes, native range Vietnam (several provinces) + Laos. Recommends group of 6+; used 3 as minimum floor (Co-Op and Aquatic Arts both cite 3+). Hardness given as 18–179 ppm (~1–10 dGH); used gH 5–15 and kH 4–12 per broader source consensus and user key data.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirms family Gastromyzontidae, order Cypriniformes, max size 5.7 cm SL, native to China/Vietnam/Cambodia (Mekong basin). IUCN Vulnerable (VU). Used as taxonomy and size cross-check.

additional.name: Aquatic Arts
additional.notes: Confirms temperature 68–75°F (20–24°C), pH 6.5–7.5, group of 3+, fast-moving water requirement. Consistent with SeriouslyFish on temperature ceiling. Used as third source confirming cool-water specialist profile.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning is typically triggered by a temporary rise in temperature to 25–26°C or by cool water changes mimicking seasonal rainfall. Eggs are deposited in gravel substrate interstices and receive no parental care. Newly hatched fry initially consume infusoria (microscopic aquatic organisms), then graduate to Artemia nauplii and microworms as they grow. Breeding is considered achievable in home aquaria given a mature, biofilm-rich setup with adequate mulm in the substrate to support early fry.
